Asking whether SSL is dull duplex seems like an odd way to frame this problem.  The "want read" and "want write" mean that the SSL state machine needs data or has negotation data to send to the peer -- not that the socket itself needs reading or writing.

It seems to me like using a selector to check if an "SSL socket" is readable has underspecified semantics unless the selector is aware of SSL and "readable" means "application data has arrived".   This is explained in the [Notes on non-blocking sockets section of the SSL module docs](https://docs.python.org/2/library/ssl.html#notes-on-non-blocking-sockets).  My understanding is that if Curio uses an "SSL socket" wrapper object, it cannot equate "the selector says the (raw) socket is readable" to "the Curio socket is readable".

It's not that simple.  Consider for example an objective function that does cross validation of a binary classifier and measures the number of times the classifier makes a classification error.  That objective function is piece-wise constant and therefore its derivative is 0 essentially everywhere. That derivative tells you nothing useful for optimization.  Many things are like this.  

But yes, if you had a function that is smooth then you could use the derivatives profitably.  But that's not really the situation in many hyper parameter optimization settings, which is the intended use for find_max_global().

This is an old bug of Visual Studio, you will not be able to make this without simplifying the network structure. If you need to make it work with Windows OS, you can use MinGW or Clang.

MinGW is fully supported by DLib but will not work with CUDA and not compatible with MSVC .libs

CLang will work with Cuda and will be compatible with MSVC, but compiling Dlib with it will require some  changes in dlib's CMakeLists.txt. I don't remember the list of changes, not trivial (but works)

Most simple way to make training - use Linux machine, or you can remove some levels from network and it will start compiling on Windows

Ah, interesting! I'll keep that in mind next time I'm poking at those
docs...

The important thing though is that there I was talking about an http
server, where putting a timeout around it would mean "shut down the server
30 seconds after it starts", which is useless; what you want is "shut down
any individual http request handler 30 seconds after it starts". The
generalization is that if your timeout argument would just impose a simple
timeout on the whole operation, you shouldn't include it because it's
redundant and distracting. But if you want to let people impose a timeout
on some internal piece of the operation, then it makes sense to have a
timeout argument, because it can't be accomplished any other way.

Another example: I could imagine it being useful sometimes in asks to have
a requests-style timeout that fires if the server is quiet for a long time;
it'd be useful in some specific situations like where the overall download
is expected to take an hour but you want to abort early if the network
drops out. That would have to be an argument. But it should probably have
some specialized name to reflect its specialized use, like idle_timeout= or
something.

@dgilland

Currently, the choice of using the repo slug as argument is deliberate (aka `<user>/<repo>`), and there are several reasons for that:

* it's to make the API simple and unified: `git create` and `git request` accept the same repo slug parameter ;
* it's not meant to be smart or automagical at this level (at least yet) ;
* it's using and managing an `origin`-free set of remotes (so basically, you leave `git-repo` managing `upstream`, `gitlab`, `github`, `bitbucket`, and `gogs` and you do whatever you want with any other remotes) ;

I agree adding an *upstream* remote when current repository is already a fork should be made simple (though, you can already do `git lab add the-team/the-project upstream`. If you're not using the `-a/--alone` switch, it'll be added to the [`all` remote for pushes](http://i.got.nothing.to/code/on/git:_how_to_setup_one_remote_with_multiple_url/). Maybe a magical command like `git target add upstream` could do that job.

Whether to use a remote name instead of a repo slug is not an idea I'm against, as I don't think it should add much extra complexity to the tool. The way I see such thing would be to extend the [`repo_slug` argument handler](https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/devel/git_repo/repo.py#L225,L239) so:

* when an argument without a `/` is given,
* if the argument exists as a remote,
* the remote matches the subcommand service type specified (i.e.: `gitlab.com` for target being a `GitlabService`)
* setup the `repo_slug`, `repo_name`, `user_name` members accordingly. 

You're welcome to join the IRC channel `#git-repo` on freenode to chat this (and your other issues) further! And also, thank you for all your reporting 😉

Working example without the `select` implementation:
```python
async def main():
    out = Channel()
    stop = asyncio.Event()
    asyncio.ensure_future(producer(out, stop))
    for _ in range(3):
        print("from out: {}".format(await out.get()))
    stop.set()

async def producer(out, stop):
    i = 0
    while not stop.is_set():
        done, pending = await asyncio.wait([stop.wait(), out.put(i)], return_when=asyncio.FIRST_COMPLETED)
        [p.cancel() for p in pending]
        if stop.is_set():
            out.close()
            break
        else:
            i += 1

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main())
loop.close()
```

Hi @brechtm,

I was looking at python-textile already. I prefer to build on that project instead of starting from scratch.

Your options:

1. I was thinking along these lines. Split up the current processing in two steps: first parse to an AST, then convert that AST to HTML.
I'd split the first part off, so rinohtype could use the AST outputted by the textile parser, transform that to a Flowable tree and have rinohtype render that.
Advantages: adding a layer of abstraction to the textile project would be a good thing for that project: with an AST as intermediate representation of the document, other renderers could be easily implemented.

2. I like this proposal, because it would mean less work on the rinohtype side of things. I'm not sure how cleanly a docutils document tree as intermediate format would map to python-textile.

3. I don't like transpilers. It feels like fixing a problem by bolting on another layer of indirection. This is definitely not the way I'm going :)

I'm deliberating between 1 and 2. Thoughts are welcome.

TL;DR executive summary:  if you care about penny rounding errors, use a real database.

Originally ofxalchemy.types.OFXNumeric stored data as string on the SQLite backend, but I abandoned that because it conflicted with setting SQL CHECK CONSTRAINT statements - most importantly to ensure that INVTRAN aggregates actually do have TOTAL set to the correct value so you can just use it instead of recalculating UNITS * UNITPRICE + COMMISSION et al. every time.

I've got plenty of OFX data where the server violates this part of the spec, and have deemed that data integrity trumps potential rounding errors on SQLite.  OFX data precision doesn't run too many decimal places, and so far I haven't run into cases where rounding errors occurred upon quantization.

I'm interested in any actual errors here, and plan to have reconciliation functions which will roll forward transactions from starting balances to catch discrepancies in ending balances... but to my mind, SQLite is mostly for testing purposes.  For real financial data with high accuracy needs, use a stricter DB back end.  I am happy with postgres.

Thank you for the suggestion. We usually turn of the splitting of reads in two groups by albacore. I think it really depends on your application whether this cut off makes any sense or not. Probably for assembly, SNP calling, diagnostics you don't want the most terrible reads, but for example for structural variants I don't mind getting evidence from lower quality reads.

Note that there is a `--minqual` flag in NanoPlot, which you could use to set your prefered cut off. I don't like setting 7 as the default here, because it's unexpected behaviour. If you present a summary file to NanoPlot each read will get plotted, unless you explicitly set the parameter to do that differently.

Cheers,
Wouter

You can already control the output file name to that resolution, but it is a bit round-about.

`extra_valid_entries` lets you add new metadata entries.
`include_in_X` lets you include existing metadata entries in the new ones you create.
`replace_metadata` lets you do that sort of replacement on metadata entries.
`output_filename` lets you define the output path name using metadata entries.

So you can use something like:
```
[epub]
add_to_extra_valid_entries:,filetitle,fileauthor
include_in_filetitle:title
include_in_fileauthor:author
add_to_replace_metadata:
 filetitle,fileauthor=>'=>
 filetitle,fileauthor=>[ ]=>_
 # space should be replaced with '_' by output_filename_safepattern, but that's how you'd do it, not \s.
output_filename: ${fileauthor}:${filetitle}${formatext}
```

```py
        # If it is C:\ then colon was encoded. Decode it back.
        url = re.sub(r"^([a-zA-Z])%3A", r"\1:", url)

        # Allow hash when loading urls. The pathname2url function
        # replaced hashes with "%23" (Issue #114).
        url = url.replace("%23", "#")

        # Allow more special characters when loading urls. The pathname2url

Hi Deepak,

Thanks for the very detailed bug report. That really helps to narrow these issues down.

I wasn't aware of this but it looks like Excel has an undocumented limit of 60,000 unique non-contiguous data validations per worksheet.

For example if you take this variation of your sample program that generates 60,000 unique data validations:

```python
import sys, random, string
from xlsxwriter import Workbook

def random_string(length=20):
    return ''.join(random.choice(string.ascii_uppercase + string.digits) for _ in range(length))


def create_file(number_of_rows):
    workbook = Workbook("large_file10.xlsx")
    sheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

    for row_index in range(0, number_of_rows):
        option = random_string()
        sheet.write(row_index, 0, row_index + 1)
        sheet.write(row_index, 1, option)
        sheet.data_validation(row_index, 1, row_index, 1, {'validate': 'list', 
                                                           'source': [option]})
    workbook.close()

if  __name__ == "__main__":
    create_file(number_of_rows=60000)
```
Generate and open this file and add one more unique data validation at the end. Save the file, close it and then re-open it. You will get the same error message as above.

Excel does support > 60,000 non-unique, non-contiguous data validations by using an optimisation for duplicate validations (after 60,000).

I'll try add the same optimization in a later release but for now is there some other workaround that you can use?

John

> I'm curious to know if there any reference which mention about this restriction else we need to find the work around using trial and error approach.

I wasn't able to find any reference to it. However, I know how to fix it. I'll try get around to it in the next week or so but I'm a little busy at the moment.

Just in case you aren't aware, if the data validation isn't different in each cell then you can apply a single data validation over a large range so you may be able to create a workaround like that.

```python
import sys, os.path, subprocess

sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(__file__)))
import kconfiglib
class KernelVersion(object):
    """Lazy evaluation of kernel version finder."""
    def __str__(self):
        return subprocess.check_output(
            ["make", "kernelversion"],
            universal_newlines=True).strip()

for k, v in {
    'ARCH': "x86_64",
    'SRCARCH': "x86",
    'KERNELVERSION': KernelVersion(),
}.items():
    if not k in os.environ:
        os.environ[k] = str(v)
```

Anyway for my purpose, the `SRCARCH`, `ARCH` and `KERNELVERSION` variables and Kconfig files are static (they do not change). I was trying to make [a tool that helps me in making a smaller kernel config](https://github.com/Lekensteyn/Kconfiglib/blob/hackery/examples/count_children.py), so the only thing that constantly changes was the generator script and (later) possibly the .config file.

If those conditions are not valid, then serialization using pickle can indeed not be used. But in the use case above, it does :smiley:.
___
The following tests are described by this Python code which were ran after reading the git tree from a SSD into memory:

 - Config: `import kconfiglib; kconfiglib.Config()`
 - Config+pickle.dump: `import kconfiglib, pickle, sys; c=kconfiglib.Config(); sys.setrecursionlimit(100000); pickle.dump(c, open("/tmp/x","wb"))`
 - pickle.load: `import kconfiglib, pickle, sys; c=pickle.load(open("/tmp/x", "rb"))`

The `bash` command is:

    for i in {0..9}; do
        time PYTHONPATH=../Kconfiglib \
          ARCH=x86_64 SRCARCH=x86 KERNELVERSION=4.2.0-rc5 \
          python -c 'import kconfiglib; kconfiglib.Config()';
    done

For an i5-560M (used in the original report and throughout development):

| test (n=10, Py3.4.10) | avg    | min    | max    | stdDev  |
| --------------------- | ------ | ------ | ------ | ------- |
| Config                | 3.611s | 3.513s | 3.76s  | 0.0871s |
| Config+pickle.dump    | 4.333s | 4.213s | 4.537s | 0.1089s |
| pickle.load           | 1.693s | 1.618s | 1.82s  | 0.0595s |

For an i7-3770 (for comparison):

| test (n=10, Py3.4.10) | avg    | min    | max    | stdDev  |
| --------------------- | ------ | ------ | ------ | ------- |
| Config                | 1.324s | 1.306s | 1.345s | 0.0138s |
| Config+pickle.dump    | 1.627s | 1.603s | 1.653s | 0.0160s |
| pickle.load           | 0.616s | 0.602s | 0.63s  | 0.0105s |

The resulting `/tmp/x` file is 7.9MiB by the way.

As you can see, (de)serialization helps a lot with reducing run time for repeated runs with the same Kconfig state, whether it is useful/applicable depends on your use case. Optimizations are probably possible, have not investigated it further.

Then I opened the monitor of mac and found that the memory of the program kept increasing and never came down. (It is more obvious if you print more `seg.length()`).

![memory1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7380327/31324753-8d8b7cc2-ac7b-11e7-8eb5-d6bbdb127a86.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7380327/31324762-b06be574-ac7b-11e7-8788-a23b0b615c31.png)

However, if I removed the code `print(seg.length())`, the memory kept nearly constant. So I thought the problem happened in `seg.length()`.
I then turned to an older library [svg.path](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/svg.path) and calculate the length with it:
```
from svg.path import CubicBezier
if __name__ == '__main__':
    file = "913.svg"
    for i in range(10000):
        paths, _, _ = svg2paths2(file)
        seg = paths[1][0]
        print(CubicBezier(seg.start, seg.control1, seg.control2, seg.end).length(error=1e-5))
```
This time I found the memory didn't keep increasing. Thus I use this method to fix the problem for the time being.

Sorry to pollute the *magic-wormhole* issue board with this, but I want to reply to @anarcat's claim.

> i am not sure croc has the same security properties as wormhole. it seems more like an unprotected pastebin than a really secure transfer system

These claims are a little unbased. While *croc* does not have the exact same security properties as *wormhole* it is secure. It uses AES-256 with pbkdf2-derived keys. It is not a pastebin, since the no content data is ever stored on the server which only acts as a relay (the server does store metadata, but this is also encrypted, and it is deleted from the server once the file transfer completes or after a specified time).

> it's also too bad that it fragments the problem space

I think this is not a good way to think about this. Having multiple solutions to the same problem is a good thing - it effectively creates a more robust general solution by providing alternatives and decentralizing the trust in a service. It also allows people building the tools to learn from one another and build from the best ideas (which is what I did when trying to create something similar to *magic-wormhole*).

Eh? I assume you are talking about Python 3, because `zlib.compress()` doesn't accept `memoryview` in Python 2 (sadly).

Anyway, it is my intent for this to work. I /thought/ that using the `y#` format unit accepted anything conforming to the buffer protocol, as https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/arg.html#strings-and-buffers says it will accept any "bytes-like object" and anything implementing the buffer protocol is a "bytes-like object." I see that CPython uses `y*` for these functions. While the docs say `y*` is recommended, they don't say there are any feature differences. But I guess there must be. Boo.

Anyway, `y*` is the superior mechanism. So the code should be changed to that. This will require changing Python 2 to `s*` so we don't have multiple code paths for a `Py_buffer` and `char *` source.

For a three-state output like that, you might want to look at using rock=-1, paper=0, scissors=1, and then change the `activation_functions` setting (in the phenotype section of the config file) from sigmoid to tanh (because tanh outputs go from -1 to 1, while sigmoid is only 0 to 1). 

Alternatively, you could add `identity` to the list of allowed activation functions, and then the network would be free to evolve outputs that are not limited to a particular output range.

However, if I understand correctly, what people will frequently do when they want three or more possible output values from a neural net is create a single bit for each possible output.  So in your case you might have three outputs, and your rps_outputs values would be something like [[1, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0], [0, 1, 0], [1, 0, 0], [0, 0, 1], [0, 0, 1]].  If you go that route. you would just need to change the `output_nodes` setting in the config file from 1 to 3.

Adding blank=True was not changing anything.
But I narrowed down the problem since my post, and applied a temporary fix.
The problem is that this boolean field is the only field (to be filled by the user) on level 2. 
So what happens is that if the user keeps the default value (for instance False), Django starts by looking at the level 2 model fields and thinks that it shouldn't save this model as there is nothing else but default values. Then it tries to save a level 3 model with a foreign key on an unsaved level 2 model. It's rather unhappy and throws the above mentioned error. 

I temporarily solved the problem by changing the booleanField for a charField with choices "true" or "false" and putting null=True, blank=False. It forces the user to pick either true or false which aren't default values and therefore guarantees that the level 2 model will be saved. 

But ideally there should be a mechanism in place for the level 2 model to be saved if it is referred to in a level 3 model.

In general, I would still strongly encourage to use the user-agent redirect loop WITH setting the RP/app cookie expiration to the id token's expiration - that way you never have a situation where a session is incorrectly up (that's a rather bad security risk).
The API mainly allows apps that need to know about users asynchronously, ahead of time, etc. - in fact both can be used at the same time as well.

1. We do have some internal doc on this at least (i.e. "auth0 setup side") at https://mana.mozilla.org/wiki/display/SECURITY/Auth0+Setup#Auth0Setup-TheAuth0managementAPIandAuth0clients though this could be improved of course

3. It depends, the advantage of the user-agent redirect loop is that it requires no additional privileges and there is a clear indication on the auth0 side of what happened (unlike with the API, at the moment at least)

5. Somewhat yes, i.e. we do have a backlog item to do the same thing through our own API instead - others have the same interest and are trying something with a project called Hydra (via @mozbhearsum)

6. We attempt to find any RP that does not follow the rules (ie that uses long sessions but never refresh the user profile in some fashion) - auth0 API is definitely a way that can work, until 5. is solved -albeit it does not give a strong indicator that the user profile is refreshed (and thus, we pick the RP up as "not following the rules" even thus it may do that)

All in all I think this should be optional (due to using a proprietary API + session refresh notification issues) with a clear indication of what it does vs the user-agent redirect - this allows users of the lib to take the best decision for their app.

That said, one thing I value very highly is backwards-compatibility. Sorry for breaking that code!

If we can extend the API a bit to make it more flexible, without breaking existing stuff and while still refactoring and keeping the code simple, then I would like to be able to handle your use case.

Do all of your objects share a common base class? Is there a way to enumerate them? I'm trying to think of what is the most common and best way to do this.

One way would be to have some global flag that disables features, e.g. disabling use of `__slots__`, disabling use of `__getnewargs_ex__`, etc.

But, disabling those features would be global across the entire encode operation, and I really don't like anything global (despite the globals that we do expose via the API) which is why I ask about the types of classes you're interesting in handling.  Reading the SO thread, it sounds like they all inherit from a common Message class?

That suggests that we might be able to have a table that disables features for specific classes and (optionally) their descendent classes. That'd at least make jp more flexible and capable of handling your use case.

Another crazy idea -- you could monkey-patch that Message class to kill the `__getstate__` method. It is python, afterall.  Yes, hacky, but that mght work.

The one thing that'll ensure that these features work for your use case is to whittle the use case down into a test case that we can add to the repo.  That way we'll never break your code because our tests will protect it. Do you think you might be able to help whittle this problem down to its bare essentials in the form of a test?

It'll probably also better help us understand the scope of the change. FWIW, it sounds doable.

Since we only use it when constructing a dict with a `type` key, I think it's preferable for the kwarg to match the key. Using `type=` as a kwarg doesn't actually cause any problems.

And actually, then you don't have to store the split planes at all, only pointers to the left/right tree nodes. Which again breaks your fixed tree node size constraint unless leaves are tiny, but if you had dynamic leaf node sizes then the whole tree would be a lot smaller. 

If you do that then it would also make sense to reorder the indices so that the roots and interior tree nodes are first, etc, for memory locality purposes.

Another idea I had was to just store the points in the interior nodes and not in the leaves, and push them onto the priority queue while traversing the tree. But that doesn't really help when you have multiple trees and the same points which are interior in one tree end up in a leaf of another tree.

Hey Shawn,

[PEP-476](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0476/) talks about the technical details of the new SSL behaviour.

The [Opting out section of PEP-476](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0476/#id29) also provides details on how to disable certificate verification.

> For users who wish to opt out of certificate verification, they can achieve this by providing the context argument to urllib.urlopen :
>
>```python
> import ssl
>
> # This restores the same behavior as before.
> context = ssl._create_unverified_context()
> urllib.urlopen("https://no-valid-cert", context=context)
> ```
> It is also possible though highly discouraged to globally disable verification by monkeypatching the ssl module:
> 
> ```python
> import ssl
> 
> ssl._create_default_https_context = ssl._create_unverified_context
> ```

Hope that helps.

Regards,
Marin

I agree, the benefits aren't visible at all on the KNN learner widget, since the only thing overridden is the `LEARNER` property. I chose KNN because I wanted to start simple.

If you take a look at the [OWSVMRegression](https://github.com/biolab/orange3/blob/master/Orange/widgets/regression/owsvmregression.py), you can see a lot more is overridden. Even further, the [OWLinearRegression](https://github.com/biolab/orange3/blob/master/Orange/widgets/regression/owlinearregression.py) and [OWLogisticRegression](https://github.com/biolab/orange3/blob/master/Orange/widgets/classify/owlogisticregression.py) widgets are to be merged, which, at first glance, appear to have even less in common.

Also, my goal is to make this mixin more generalised, so it could be used for any widget, not just learners. For example, the classic tree viewer widget will eventually have to be merged into one single widget that supports both classification and regression trees. The way I solved this in [OWPythagoreanTree](https://github.com/biolab/orange3/blob/master/Orange/widgets/visualize/owpythagorastree.py#L360-L385) was with a method that would detect which type of tree was present and then call the appropriate methods to handle the tree type. This meant all the method handlers had to be in the same class when really they are addressing different problems, so that was over 200 LOC for just handlers ([link](https://github.com/biolab/orange3/blob/master/Orange/widgets/visualize/owpythagorastree.py#L387-L579)).

I believe this approach would generalise well to other widgets and would make code, where there are a lot of handlers, more readable.

If I understand correctly, the issue is that the client (Jira) maintains a long lasting LDAP connection.  The proxy makes a long lasting connection to the server, but the server (AD) doesn't want to maintain long connections, so after some time it drops the proxy-to-server connection.  The client (Jira) is unaware.

The thing to do would be when you create a `clientConnector` on your LDAP proxy protocol (derived from `ProxyBase`), you would need to create instances of `LDAPClient` that had their `connectionLost()` methods hooked up to something.  You'd probably want it to call `queue()` on your `ProxyBase` protocol and have it send an unsolicited response [1] to the client to warn it that the connection was about to drop, and ten break the connection with `self.transport.close()`.

It sounds a lot more complicated than it is.  Simply put, when the proxy-to-server connection closes unexpectedly, the proxy should notify the client and close its connection.

[1] https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4511#section-4.4

This would be pretty easy to setup.  We'd just need to split the check on [this line](https://github.com/Parsely/streamparse/blob/f4ec2383277fcc5172313c2585d23b653f153306/streamparse/storm/bolt.py#L366) out into its own method called `BatchingBolt.is_batch_ready()` (or something like that).  We could then just have the default implementation be:

```python
def is_batch_ready(self):
    return self._tick_counter > self.ticks_between_batches and self._batches
```

Then in your `ThresholdBatchingBolt` subclass you would just need to override that one method as:

```python
def is_batch_ready(self):
    if self._batches:
        num_tuples = sum(len(batch) for batch in itervalues(self._batches))
        return (num_tuples >= self.batch_threshold or 
                super(ThresholdBatchingBolt, self).is_batch_ready())
    else:
        return False
```

That'll give you an implementation that will fallback on time if the size threshold isn't met, so you just need to make sure you set `ticks_between_batches` high enough.

It sounds like your components simply aren't doing enough work compared to the serialization overhead.  You can fix that in a couple ways:

1.  Have you spout batch your tuples into larger tuples. For example, we currently use a spout in production that pulls things out of Kafka and chunks them of 100. This reduces our serialization overhead by a lot, and the downstream components just need to be adjusted so that they expect tuples containing lists, and iterate through the lists.
2.  Collapse components you currently have separated into single components.  The more components you have, the more you're stuck dealing with serialization overhead.  You especially want to move any filtering (if you do that) into the spout itself if you're trying to achieve maximum throughput, since then you never have to deal with the serialization overhead at all.

Thanks, @sushobhana.  Yes, the plan has been to migrate the general features of aperture-photometry functionality from `photutils` to `regions` (and then `regions` to `astropy` core).  This has been on my to-do list, but hasn't been high priority (I've also been hoping `regions` would go into `astropy` sooner than later).  Thanks for your work on improving `regions`!

I think all of the general functionality of aperture-photometry has already been moved to `regions`, including the low-level Cython `geometry` functions and the `Mask` and `BoundingBox` classes (although with some small differences).  However, I am strongly not in favor of deprecating the `photutils` Aperture classes to be replaced by the `regions` PixelRegion/SkyRegions classes or changing the `aperture_photometry` function.  Instead, I was envisioning that the `regions` classes would be used as Mixin classes in the Aperture classes to replace the methods in common (i.e. the underlying geometry/Mask machinery), but keeping the Aperture-specific methods.  One huge difference between them (beside API differences) is the Aperture classes are usually used to define *multiple* apertures, all with the same shape but different positions, within one object (i.e. `positions` is an array of positions).  Removing that functionality doesn't make any sense to me.  IIRC, it was decided long ago that the `regions` objects would be singular (one shape, one position).

Some other quick thoughts:

* Changing to the regions `geometry` classes is a trivial change of the imports (they are identical copies)

* The `ApertureMask` class is called `Mask` in regions.  IMHO, `Mask` seems too general a name (perhaps `RegionMask` is better?)

*  The `BoundingBox` class in photutils has a very useful `plot` convenience method that I would very much like to keep (for interactive work) that is currently not in `regions`.

#291 has added support for passing custom `ssl_context`. 

After geopy 1.14.0 is released (that'll be pretty soon), for anyone affected by this I suggest to do the following:
- If you're still on an older version of Python (before 2.7.9 and 3.4.3), please consider upgrading. Otherwise a warning will be issued stating that TLS verification doesn't happen.
- If you ended up using `scheme='http'`, consider switching back to the default `https` scheme along with passing a custom SSL context.
- If you were monkey-patching `urlopen` (as in the comment by @tmarthal above), please stop that, and use the following instead:

      import certifi
      import ssl
      import geopy.geocoders
      ctx = ssl.create_default_context(cafile=certifi.where())
      geopy.geocoders.options.default_ssl_context = ctx

from raven.contrib.django.client import DjangoClient


class GDPRCompliantDjangoClient(DjangoClient):

    def get_user_info(self, request):
        user_info = {}
        user = getattr(request, 'user', None)

        if user is None:
            return user_info

        authenticated = user.is_authenticated

        if not authenticated:
            return user_info
        user_info['id'] = user.pk
        user_info['username'] = user.username

        return user_info
```

	import logging
	logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

	@asyncio.coroutine
	def read_or_raise(reader, n=-1):
	    """
	    Read a given byte number from Stream. NoDataException is raised if read gives no data
	    :param reader: reader adapter
	    :param n: number of bytes to read
	    :return: bytes read
	    """
	    data = yield from reader.read(n)
	    if (n > 0) and (len(data) != n):
		logger.fatal("FATAL: Number of bytes does not match")
	    if not data:

Remettons les choses dans le contexte d'un dev logiciel classique.

On ajoute un test :

- avant ou après (le débat n'est pas là) qu'une fonctionnalité ait été ajoutée. Dans ce cas c'est dans `master`.

- pour mettre en lumière un bug du logiciel. Dans ce cas on commite le test dans une feature branch, et dans cette même branche on continue à fixer le bug. Ensuite on merge la feature branch dans `master` introduisant une nouvelle fonctionnalité et les tests qui vont avec.

Donc on ne devrait pas à avoir à ignorer des tests.

Nous on est dans un cas où on avait des tests historiques qui ont été cassés par de nouvelles fonctionnalités. On aurait pu, par ordre de préférence, les corriger, les supprimer, les commenter ou les ignorer.

> When I say Table should inherit from query, I mean that Table is just SELECT * FROM table, so any operations that you perform on Table should be perform-able on Query too. Just like Builder analysis, the node doesn't have to be the original table query but can be an arbitrary query.

In fact, I've suffered a lot with that convention in the past. There are parts of the backend that make that kind of assumption, and it's really confusing. For example, if you allow it to be an arbitrary query, you could do a join to another table, so your `Table` node is actually querying two tables, and some mismatching arises. As currently there's no need for that inheritance, I'd suggest to keep `Table` as simple as possible and reconsider inheritance later. What do you think?

@be5invis Read is correct. The workflow you need is the following. Let's assume you have 4 source .ttf fonts: fonts A1.ttf, A2.ttf, A3.ttf A4.ttf.  

1. Use the `ttx -t` tool from the fontTools package on each of the A1.ttf-A4.ttf fonts, to export into .ttx XML files those SFNT tables from these fonts which do NOT involve any glyph ID references and that you intend to keep identical in your final .ttc. For example, you might want to do `ttx -t name -t head -t 'OS/2' -t hhea A1.ttf` etc. You'll end up with four .ttx files: A1.ttx-A4.ttx. Keep those. 

2. Use `pyftmerge` (the tool from fontTools using `merge.py`) to merge the fonts A1.ttf-A4.ttf into one large B1.ttf font that will automatically merge all SFNT tables of the single fonts, including the `glyf` table (and all other glyph-dependent tables like `hmtx` etc.), and will automatically rename all the glyphs by appending suffixes, and will renumber the glyph ids in all the SFNT tables. 

3. Use the `pyftsubset` tool (fontTools using `subset.py`) to take font B1.ttf and create four .ttf fonts ("fonts C1.ttf-C4.ttf") where each will contain the glyph complement of each of the destination subfonts (for each Cx.ttf output font, you need to supply pyftsubset with a list of glyph names, using the NEW glyph naming scheme that was created by pyftmerge). That will keep the glyph names devised by pyftmerge in each of the subsetted Cx.ttf fonts, but will of course "lower" the glyph IDs so each .ttf will have glyph IDs starting from 0. 

4. Use the `ttx -t` tool on each of the fonts C1.ttf-C4.ttf to export those SFNT tables which involve the glyph IDs and that you want to keep separate in each subfont into XML files. Here, you might use `ttx -t cmap -t GSUB -t GPOS -t post AC.ttf`. This will result in files C1.ttx-C4.ttx. Move these .ttx files into a new folder. 

5. Take the resulting files C1.ttx-C4.ttx and on each one use the `ttx -m` tool to merge the font B1.ttf into them (`ttx -m B1.ttf C1.ttx`, `ttx -m B1.ttf C2.ttx` etc.). Since ttx operates on glyph names, it will take the font B1.ttf and on each run will write into it SFNT tables that are found in the C1.ttx-C4.ttx files. It will use the glyph names to match the glyphs, and will renumber the glyph IDs in those tables to match the glyph IDs of the B1.ttf font. You will end up with fonts C1.ttf-C4.ttf which will have the same `glyf` table (and whatever else tables you did not export into .ttx in step 4), but each will have a different set of subsetted tables that you did include in step 4. Move those C1.ttf-C4.ttf files into a new folder. 

6. Use the `ttx -m` tool to merge these new C1.ttf-C4.ttf fonts with the A1.ttx-A4.ttx files you created in step 1: `ttx -m C1.ttf A1.ttx` etc. You'll end up with new fonts A1.ttf-A4.ttf which have the original non-glyph-dependent tables, unique "subsetted" tables that were created by pyftsubset, and identical combined tables which you left intact. 

7. Finally, run `otf2otc` on the new A1.ttf-A4.ttf font files to create a final .ttc. 

I hope it makes sense to you :) 

Best,
Adam

Yes I know, but you have a misunderstanding.
First, what GFW knows is only the (ip, port) we connected to. No protocol, no domain info, no any other things.

When GFW camouflage as a probe, it will connect to (74.125.239.145, 443) and get certificate info.
BUT, GFW DOESN'T KNOW ANY OTHER INFO. In this case, it only knows visit this IP can got a Google certificate, which certify the response server is www.google.com, but it CAN'T proof the relationship between 74.125.239.145 and www.google.com or Google compnay, even it is a real Google IP address.

When GFW connect to the server running shadowsocks on port 443, it will also try to connect to (123.123.123.123, 443), if shadowsocks found this request cannot be parsed, it may proxy the request to (74.125.239.145, 443), and then returns google certificate again. GFW ALSO CANNOT verify the relationship between 123.123.123.123 and www.google.com or Google company, the only things it can do is ignore the certificate check, assuming it's a real Google IP address, and get the server response.

So I think GFW can verify your certificate BUT CAN'T detect if you are a real HTTPS service provider. Not because we are using self-signed certificate, but because any connect via (ip, port) can't proof you are a real service provider (normally certificate is issued to hostname, so (hostname, port) can proof).

Since we have peroxided to a real HTTPS service provider, if the content we returned after ignore check is legal,  GFW have no reason to block our IP because every HTTPS site responding like us.

Looks like a known issue. Here is a workaround to unblock your stack until we fix it on our side.

You need to trigger a redeployment of API resources to unblock the stack. Here is how you do it. 

* If you use an external Swagger file, just add a comma, space or an extra character that will change the Swagger's SHA. Deploy your stack now, and it should work.

* If you use an implicit API (the ones defined using `Events` property on AWS::Serverless::Function), you can add a new dummy API to your `Events` and redeploy. Example:
```
RestApiFunction:
     Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
     Properties:
       Handler: index.handler
       Runtime: nodejs4.3
       CodeUri: s3://bucket/code.zip
       Events:
         AddItem:
           Properties:
             Method: get
             Path: /add
           Type: Api
        DummyApiForWorkaround:
           Properties:
             Method: get
             Path: /some-dummy-api
           Type: Api    
```

Once the deployment is successful, you can revert the workaround and future deployments should succeed.

I am not sure the documentation wording is the best, but I am not the most familiar with AAI and SAML definitions, so other people should comment on this too. In particular `adapter` is not used anywhere in the project. 

There are some small typos:
```diff
- The might be a mismatch between
+ There might be a mismatch between
@@
- limited SAML implementation. the proxy
+ limited SAML implementation. The proxy 
@@
- Support for a NameID format Emailaddress
+ Support for NameID of format 'emailAddress'
@@
- If an SP used friendly attribute names instead of OIDs that implementaiton should fix this. In the meantime
+ # I think this is irrelevant to the section
@@
- add a second attribute profile
+ set different attribute_profile values for the backend and frontend
```

_Support for NameID of format `emailAddress`_ is not something that should be documented in SATOSA, but `pysaml2`. It is not a SATOSA configuration option, but an `idp` configuration and as such based on pysaml's capabilities.

Setting the `attribute_profile` configuration option is not _adding_ an attribute profile. To be able to map a protocol to itself (thus rename attributes) one needs to make sure the front and back end `attribute_profile` configuration _values_ do not clash - ie the values need to be different in order to be able to differentiate between the incoming and outgoing attributes and configure them differently in `internal_attributes.yaml` config.

```python
"dateRangeType": [
    "CUSTOM_DATE"
]
```

The `dateRangeType` field is not a collection, you should specify it like so:

```python
"dateRangeType": "CUSTOM_DATE"
```

Okay, I understand what's going on now.

Lesson learned: binding a C function to return type `<PyObject*>` then casting that to type `<object>` increments the reference count of the object one more than if the function were bound to return type `<object>`.

For example, suppose you bind a function two different ways
```python
cdef extern from "my_file.h":
    PyObject* MyFuncPtr "MyFunc"()
    object MyFuncObj "MyFunc"()
```
then
```python
PyObject *obj
object val

# this
obj = MyFuncPtr()
val = <object>obj

# is equivalent to
val = <object>MyFuncPtr()

# and increments the reference count one more than this
val = MyFuncObj()
```
Everything related to this should now be fixed.

@MarquitoForrest Your understanding is exactly right.  What I said in that StackOverflow post is a temporary workaround.  The correct solution will be for Pillow to be linked to Tcl and Tk in exactly the same way as Python's own `_tkinter` binary is linked; then `ImageTk.PhotoImage()` will "Just Work".  It is very frustrating for everyone, but that's just how it goes sometimes, and we try our best to help in little bits where possible.

I saw now that I made an error in the StackOverflow answer I made.  I have now fixed that mistake, perhaps try reading through it again to see if it makes more sense?

I'll try to explain a bit more, to perhaps help you get a bit further.  The `otool -L` command tells you how a binary file has been linked on the system.  There are several ways that so-called dynamic linking can be set up.  In the Pillow (PIL) distribution, there is one particular binary file causing us all these problems (in _my_ conda env named "py35"; yours will differ):

```
~/anaconda/envs/py35/lib/python3.5/site-packages/PIL/_imagingtk.so
```
This is where the support for Tkinter in Pillow comes from.  Unfortunately for us, when this was compiled it was linked to a framework version of Tcl and Tk.  On OS X, the linking path is often saved in the final binary.  To see what these paths are, you can use `otool`:

```
$ otool -L _imagingtk.so
```

(Assuming you're in that folder).  So what you see will be framework paths.  This doesn't work because in the conda env, _Tkinter_ itself was **not** linked to a framework, but rather, the Tcl and Tk binaries inside the conda env itself.  You can see that also with `otool`, but run it on the `_tkinter.so` binary:

```
otool -L ~/anaconda/envs/py35/lib/python3.5/lib-dynload/_tkinter.so
```
With Python 3, the name of the binary includes the Python version, so it may look something like `_tkinter.cpython-35m-darwin.so`.

So, one way to fix the issue, is to simply change the linking path directly inside the Pillow binary.  For this, you can use a tool called `install_name_tool`.  What I recommend for you, is to change the two framework paths for Tcl and Tk, to be exactly the same as they appear in the `_tkinter.so` binary.  This _should_ work.  In my case, I just used `@rpath/libtcl8.5.dylib` and `@rpath/libtk8.5.dylib`, but remember, my venv uses a Tcl 8.5 version, whereas it seems yours is using 8.4.  You have to match what is in your conda environment.

@asmeurer:

> I think I understand what you are suggesting here now. You are calling it "system" because there would be literally one conda installed on a given system.

Exactly! :-)  Conda isn't necessarily managed by itself, but when it is it's a system install.  All that ever has is `conda`, `conda-build`, `conda-env`, and so on.

To put it another way, right now we have this concept of a single package that can only be installed in the root environment: `conda`.  I want to remove that special case and let environments specify what types of packages they'll accept, then put it on the package to use the correct meta data to get into that environment.

I'm renaming it from "root" to "system" to avoid any confusion by having an entirely separate concept.

I (hopefully) made a work-around for now by re-installing an earlier conda version and dis-allowing updating conda by adjusting the `.condarc` file as described in the [User guide - automatically updating conda](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/configuration/use-condarc.html#update-conda-automatically-auto-update-conda) and [User guide - disallowing installation of specific packages](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/configuration/use-condarc.html#disallow-installation-of-specific-packages-disallow)

Personally, I'm not sure there's a compelling reason to support offering different behaviors on import and when setting attributes directly. If this code:
```python
person = Person({'first_name': 'John'})
```
and this code:
```python
person = Person()
person.first_name = 'John'
```
can cause different results, that seems like a huge, huge opportunity for user error. The majority of users  *will* expect these two snippets to behave exactly the same, and thus will use these two forms interchangeably. The confusion and subtle bugs surrounding this will by far outweigh the benefits of the additional extra functionality.

Adding Damian,

Those issues are flagging Asp.net binaries in two different locations.

1. The runtime "store". This location is cumulative and things in here are used by applications based on exact version match. If anything in here is getting used, the application using it needs to be updated.

2. NugetFallbackFolder, this is a cache of nuget packages. Similar to above, the only way the version there will be used is if an application references it and in that case, the application needs to be updated.

The warning in the documetation you linked is exactly what you are experiencing:

> the invocation order of decorated methods of the same type is not guaranteed

In some cases, `make_instance` runs first, and in some cases `create_stuff` runs first.

You can get around this by overwriting `make_instance` on your schema, then calling `super().make_instance()` when you want.

    @post_load
    def make_instance(self, data):
        result = super().make_instance(data)

        # result will be a model instance here

        return result

@kirsle What do you think about sorting of this edge case? The current algorithm developed in this branch **_makes sure_** the super-catchall (`*`) to be the last position. However its close cousin (`[*]`) is not taken care of. `[*]` precedes the 2nd trigger below (`[*] text [*]`) because it has lesser number of stars. 
```
+ [*]
- match all 1
+ [*] text [*]
- match with text
+ *
- match all 2
```
The ideal sorting that I can think of is below. The rationale behind is the last trigger has larger coverage of messages than the second last. The difference is empty message, even though empty messages doesn't match the last template (due to Regex). 
```
+ [*] text [*]
- match with text
+ *
- match all 2
+ [*]
- match all 1
```

@wholmgren I hadn't realized the distinction between future and deprecation or pending deprecation warnings until now. 

### TL;DR:
According to my read of the [PEP](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0565/) and [Python Warnings docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/warnings.html#warning-categories), I think we should use `FutureWarning`.

### more detail
Since PVLib is more like a developer toolbox, and less likely to be consumed by another application, it seems appropriate to issue verbose warnings. I think until Python-3.7 becomes more widespread, most users with Python-3.6 won't see deprecation or pending deprecation warnings unless they have turned warning filters off. If PEP-565 is backported to Python-3.6 then we can reconsider.

I also think we need guidelines in the contributor documentation that establish our procedures and some terms such as:
* how many releases should a `FutureWarning` be used before an item is deprecated?
* shall a deprecated feature be kept in a deprecated state forever, to maintain backwards compatibility?
* should a list of future and deprecated features be kept somewhere?
* what annotations are used in the documentation like `.. versionadded:` and `.. deprecated:` and are ad hoc warnings also used?
* when if ever is `PendingDeprecationWarning` used?

Pandas seems like a similar toolbox to PVLib, so I looked at it to see their MO. AFAICT: they seem to take the view that when new features will deprecate old ones, there are 3 stages:
1. both exist and are accepted (`FutureWarning`)
2. the feature is about to be deprecated (`PendingDeprecationWarning`)
3. the feature is deprecated, no longer in use and could be removed at any time (`DeprecationWarning`)

#### Pandas
I searched through the [https:/github.com/pandas-dev](Pandas codebase) and [documents](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/) and here's my guess:

1. future warnings are used first, they indicate:

    - that in the future this command, argument, feature, api, syntax, etc. will change to something new
    - they are used when a new feature is added that would break backwards compatibility
    - sphinx documentation is annotated with [`.. versionadded:: x.y.z`](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/contributing.html?highlight=versionadded#documenting-your-code), for example see the [`join` argument in `concat`](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/text.html?highlight=futurewarning#concatenating-a-series-and-an-indexed-object-into-a-series-with-alignment)
    - sphinx documentation is annotated with [`.. deprecated: x.y.z`](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/contributing.html?highlight=deprecation#backwards-compatibility), for example see [`groupby.take` `convert` negative indices flag](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/generated/pandas.core.groupby.DataFrameGroupBy.take.html?highlight=deprecated%20since)
    - ad hoc warnings and `.. warning: blah` annotations are used in the documentation
    - after X releases, the feature is deprecated
    - items are listed in [pandas issue 6581](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/6581)

2. `PendingDeprecationWarning` is used infrequently, perhaps because it is only used for 1 or 2 releases prior to actual deprecation

3. features that have actually been deprecated use deprecation warnings

    - sphinx documentation is annotated with `.. deprecated:: x.y.z`
    - warnings are used ad hoc if the feature may be removed in a future version
    - items are listed in [pandas issue 13777](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/13777)

Pandas provides [decorators to deprecated functions and kwargs](../../../pandas-dev/pandas/blob/master/pandas/util/_decorators.py#L9) which defaults to `FutureWarning`, and their [contributor docs also say to issue a future warning](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/contributing.html?highlight=deprecation#backwards-compatibility).

>>> from z3 import *

>>> x = z3.Int('x')
>>> p = z3.Bool('p')

> Let's go back to using getter and setter here. Because we might be able to decide what's Pythonic or not for us. But how users will import and use that global is another story. We should avoid this non-obvious pitfall; it's also a hard one to figure out.

> Another way to do this would be to add code to `cuda_convnet.py` and `dnn.py` which replaces those Layer classes with dummy wrappers around `Conv2DLayer` when the libraries are unavailable.

I don't think that's a good idea. If you're using `Conv2DCCLayer` in your code, i.e. explicitly instructing Lasagne to use the `cuda_convnet` implementation, it should not quietly go and use a different one if that one isn't available, it should fail.

Welcome, and thank you for the PR!

> I understand, though, that just implementing a nonlinearity is not good enough to make the cut

Indeed! It also needs tests so the coverage stays at 100%, and must be added to the `nonlinearities.rst` files so it shows up in the documentation. See the changeset of the closely related ELU (https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/518/files) and scaled tanh (https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/430/files).

See http://lasagne.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/development.html#how-to-contribute on how to build the documentation locally, and how to run the test suite locally. This allows you to check if everything looks fine and coverage is at 100%.

Besides, there are some other issues with your PR:
* The reference must be referenced with `[1]_`, not `[1]`
* The reference should include all authors
* `elu` is a special case of `SELU` now -- we could implement it as such, but actually it may not be worth the effort since `elu` is just a single line of code. We did the same when introducing scaled tanh; there was no point in making `tanh` a `ScaledTanh` instance.
* it's not clear what values to use for the two parameters, I'd suggest to add a `selu` instance with the parameters suggested by the authors (https://github.com/bioinf-jku/SNNs/blob/49c5298/selu.py#L23-L24). I.e., after defining the class, add `selu = SELU(..., ...)`, give it a docstring as we did here: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/6eb2f86/lasagne/nonlinearities.py#L223-L227, and reference it from the `SELU` docstring as we did here: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/6eb2f86/lasagne/nonlinearities.py#L204
* The implementation is not in line with the docstring and paper (`>=` vs `>`). Take the `elu` implementation as a starting point.
* `SELU` should come directly after `elu` in both the code and documentation since they're related.

> It complicates the code quite a bit, unfortunately

If we only allow plain inputs, lists of inputs and dicts of inputs (no nesting), it's fairly easy. Otherwise it involves some recursive function in the base class constructor and in `get_output()`.

> It's weird to have `Layer` derive from `BaseLayer` because the former is the most "generic" name.

In a perfect world, `Layer` would be called `SimpleLayer` or `BasicLayer` and all single-input layers would subclass it. We can't rename `Layer` now, so we either need a new name for the base class or `Layer` needs to support multiple inputs and then constrain it again in a way that subclasses can relax. We can also try to find a better name for `BaseLayer`.

> Also, how common is `isinstance(..., Layer)` in user code, really?

Maybe that's really something we shouldn't care too much about.

> Intuitively, I'd think it should always pass `**kwargs` to its child layers, but I have a vague feeling there may be a case when the user wouldn't want it to. Is there?

It must always be passed on, it's bad we overlooked this. If somebody uses the `CustomRecurrentLayer` with dropouts anywhere, they will never be disabled at test time. A fix would be welcome!

Do all callers of `_numeric_stats` deal with that correctly? https://github.com/ibmdbanalytics/ibmdbpy/blob/a6e4d283bfdaf835861ec4a9c25517a839d7cd88/ibmdbpy/statistics.py#L786 doesn't looks like it does, neither does https://github.com/ibmdbanalytics/ibmdbpy/blob/a6e4d283bfdaf835861ec4a9c25517a839d7cd88/ibmdbpy/statistics.py#L645. https://github.com/ibmdbanalytics/ibmdbpy/blob/3148eca771fc5a1271e47d36ee3e54f7630beb43/ibmdbpy/statistics.py#L833 and https://github.com/ibmdbanalytics/ibmdbpy/blob/3148eca771fc5a1271e47d36ee3e54f7630beb43/ibmdbpy/statistics.py#L815 already have workarounds in place to handle non-iterable return values of `_numeric_stats` since 76109b55d07e63efb5ec0a7cb3a241043de55fa9, but I'm really not a fan of having every caller work around undocumented behaviour of `_numeric_stats`. Indeed, [Eigen doesn't](http://eigen.tuxfamily.org/bz/show_bug.cgi?id=747) (but might someday).  For Eigen, it should make a copy when the reference type is non mutable (e.g. `Eigen::Ref<const MatrixXd>`).  For mutable types (`Eigen::Ref<MatrixXd>`) obviously it needs to fail.

Making it work should be pretty easy: you'd just need to add a couple of "return false;" in `static EigenConformable<row_major> conformable(const array &a)`: one in the `if (dims == 2) { ... }` case if either stride is negative, and another after that if the 1D stride is negative.

The same commit also makes mention of always using `PyErr_Fetch` and `PyErr_Restore` in pairs instead of simply decrementing the refcount. But I don't think this is necessary. Python's docs include examples where `type, value, trace` are simply decremented (e.g. [here](https://docs.python.org/3/faq/extending.html#how-do-i-tell-incomplete-input-from-invalid-input) toward the end of the second code block). Avoiding the `PyErr_Restore`/`PyErr_Clear` combo definitely simplifies `error_already_set`, and it looks like that's fine with the CPython API.

@alex,

The missing symbols for [`SSLv2_client_method`](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/1646#issuecomment-163343227) and [`SSLv3_server_method`](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/1646#issuecomment-265113738) are kind of par for the course after POODLE and some of the other attacks. Distros started configuring OpenSSL with `no-ssl2 no-ssl3`, and it removed the symbols.

Around that same time, OpenSSL defaulted to a no-ssl2 no-ssl3, too. They completely removed SSLv2, so there's no way to get it back. I believe SSLv3 is available, but you have to configure with `enable-ssl3`. In both cases, OpenSSL did not make it easy on folks. They could have disabled the protocols but left the symbols so that `SSLv2_client_method` returned `NULL` instead of an actual method. 

For a good analysis, see [undefined symbol: SSLv2_client_method](http://stackoverflow.com/q/35880228/608639) on Stack Overflow. It talks about the bug reports, the commits, and how to work around them.

I think the problem is Python (but its a very weak blame). Python should not be using protocols like SSLv2 and SSLv3 by default. At compile time, they should be checking `<openssl/opensslconf.h>` and looking for the macro `OPENSSL_NO_SSL2` and `OPENSSL_NO_SSL3`. If they choose to enable SSLv2 and SSLv3, then they should locate the symbols dynamically.

@sannek8552's [`OPENSSL_sk_num`](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/1646#issuecomment-265113738) mystery to me at the moment.

-----

Maybe you cold do the following so that things will "just work" for users:

1. Create a new C/C++ shared object
2. Add ***weak*** symbols for `SSLv2_client_method` and `SSLv3_server_method`
3. Let `SSLv2_client_method` and `SSLv3_server_method` return `NULL`
4. Link certbot to your shim

That should have the same effect as disabling SSLv2 and SSLv3 while allowing runtime linking.

`target A B C is required` ==> `step A` is triggered to produce `A`, `B`, `C`

or 

`target A B C is required` ==> `step A` is triggered to produce `A`, `step A` is triggered to produce `B`, `step A` is triggered to produce `C`
 
because either could be correct, we do

`target A B C is required` ==> `step A` is triggered to produce `A` 

Then `step A` is triggered to produce `B` if `step A` does not produce `B`.

@giulioungaretti 
The stacktrace I got did not contain any qcodes code. The problem is that the `station.snapshot()` returns non-serializable data if `self.instrument` contains something non-serializable (following the steps above).

The problem occurs because
```
           if attr == 'instrument' and self._instrument:
```
returns False (`self._instrument` is `None`)
So we enter
```
elif hasattr(self, attr):
```
with `attr` equal to `instrument` which will put the `instrument` attribute in the snapshot (and this was non-serializable)

> Livestreamer has a ton of options that we can configure in terms of stream retries, segment retries, timeouts, thread count, the *-live-edge options etc., all of which can help a TON with reducing dropouts....

youtube-dl also has many options, though I don't know if it has all of those.

I think there are two ways of looking at these projects:

1.  As some kind of all-in-one approach that can not only get the stream links from the web sites, but also download them and pass them through to players, etc.
2.  As simple tools to get the URL to the actual stream data and pass that to a player like VLC.

I think--hope, anyway--that as players like VLC improve, these projects can move more toward the second approach, delegating downloading and playing to VLC.  (VLC is also extremely configurable; just have a look at `vlc --full-help`).  It's a lot of work just keeping up with the streaming sites' churn, so letting VLC handle as much as possible seems like a good idea.

> Most of the richness of these projects is in the plugins' area (the sauce that make different sites to work with the main agnostic tool).

Yes, I think you're absolutely right about that.  That's the point I've been trying to make, but you made it more succinctly.  :)

> Maybe there's a way to adapt youtube-dl sites' plugins to streamlink and help cross-polination and broad of supported sites?

I'm not sure exactly what you mean.  If you mean to port plugins that only exist in livestreamer to youtube-dl, this could be done, but they would need to be rewritten in the process.  Of course, I think it would be worth it in the long-term, and that's the point I've been trying to make.  :)

If you mean to make some kind of bi-directional plugin API layer that translates between each project's plugins, that would not be practical.  The way the projects are written is wildly different.  youtube-dl is also a much, much larger, more complex project.

What I've been trying to do is show why it would be better for the long-term if the efforts put in here were simply moved to youtube-dl.  There is a huge community over there, and there's no risk of the authors disappearing or going idle like has happened with livestreamer.  There may be some features that only exist in livestreamer, but they could be added to youtube-dl.  

The projects are fundamentally similar in purpose--that one has historically focused on "downloading videos" while the other has focused on "streaming video" is an implementation detail that is already blurring, since youtube-dl has some support for Twitch and other streaming sites.  Already I can run something like `vlc $(youtube-dl -g twitch.tv/streamer)` and open streams directly in VLC, getting the URL with youtube-dl, and it handles Twitch VODs too.  And youtube-dl's distribution system makes it so easy to update to keep up with site churn...

As great as livestreamer has been, I struggle to see what its purpose and niche are in the long-term.  I'd just hate to see effort go to waste when it could be put into something that would have more value and longevity.

It would help if we could more clearly define why livestreamer needs to exist as compared to youtube-dl, and then we could talk about whether it makes sense to keep livestreamer going or port features to youtube-dl.

Of course, if y'all want to keep working on livestreamer, it's up to you.  :)

A potentially serious issue I just realized: this interacts a lot with #54 and #32. Basically the question is: if we have automagic locking inside `Stream`, then should it be a regular `Lock` or a `StrictFIFOLock`? Right now they're equivalent, but if we ever get a smarter scheduler or relax our fairness guarantees then they won't be. Why does this matter? Suppose you have some sans-io protocol wrapper kind of method, like:
```python
async def send_message(self, msg):
    encoded = self._sansio_conn.encode_message(msg)
    await self._stream.send_all(encoded)
```
If `send_all` uses a `StrictFIFOLock`, then this is always correct: we know that the data will eventually be written to the stream in the same order as the calls to `_sansio_conn.encode_message`. However, this may be inefficient: e.g. if we have a fancy scheduler and some task overruns its time slice, then not only will it get punished by being made to sleep for an appropriate amount of time, but any tasks that get stuck behind it in the `StrictFIFOLock` acquisition queue will also end up being punished. This would be bad. It would be sorta-possible to work around: just put a regular `Lock` around the whole method body, and if everyone holds this lock before calling `send_all` then the internal `StrictFIFOLock` will never be contended and thus become harmless. But I don't like this; the problem and workaround are *really* obscure.

OTOH if `Stream.send_all` internally uses a regular `Lock` that doesn't guarantee strict FIFO fairness, then the above code is *not* necessarily correct. In particular, suppose we have a protocol with something like a sequence number in each message. Then we could have the following sequence:

1. task A calls `encoded = _sansio_conn.send_message(msg)` and gets back encoded message number 1, then blocks waiting to acquire the send lock
2. task B calls `encoded = _sansio_conn.send_message(msg)` and gets back encoded message number 2, then blocks waiting to acquire the send lock
3. task B wakes up, and sends message number 2
4. task A wakes up, and sends message number 1

And now our protocol is broken. Again, the solution would be to put a `Lock` around the whole `send_message` method.

No worries, I'm glad it works. `make infra` can directly read the environment variables because it runs the stack (i.e., the Python processes) directly on the local machine, not in Docker.

The problem is that inside the LocalStack main Docker container we're using Python 2.7. Therefore, if we run the lambda code durectly in the main container, it fails with a syntax error.

The configuration LAMBDA_EXECUTOR=docker causes LocalStack to run the lambdas in a _separate_ Docker container (where we can switch between python versions).

One solution would be to upgrade python from 2.7 to 3.x in the main Docker image. That would probably break some existing tests because python 3 is not fully backward compatible with 2.x (e.g., `print foo` vs `print(foo)`). However, it's probably better to have 2.x tests failing rather than 3.x tests failing.

It seems as if the indexes are incorrect when sampling from prioritized replay when the memory is full. The sum trees don't use ring buffers and the indexes obtained from these when sampling refer to a specific index in the _value array of the sum trees. The observations and other ringbuffers on the other hand will change the mappings of indexes when full. E.g. accessing observations[0] when the memory is full won't necessarily give you an observation from index 0 in the ringbuffers data array.  The effect of this is that all priorities will refer to incorrect experiences when the memory is full. 

For example, Instead of doing: `terminal0 = self.terminals[idx - 2]`
you could access the observations and other ringbuffer storage from the data arrays. E.g.: `terminal0 = self.terminals.data[idx - 2]`

I'm not entirely sure if this might cause other bugs but the current implementation is incorrect. I have also compared this to the open ai baselines implementation and these implementations differ since the storage is just a normal list and not a ringbuffer in the baselines implementation. In other words they avoid this issue by accessing the array "directly ".

I was having numerous issues with this under Python 2.7 (32-bit).

* `shebang` never matched anything due to mixed slashes, case differences, and 'bin' vs. 'Scripts'
* the `new_shebang` was breaking things (CLI tools were creating themselves as (for example) `pip.exe`, and calling `pip-script.py`, but if the shebang in `pip-script.py` didn't point to a valid windows exe, it failed).

  I modified the fixup_scripts function as follows and everything seems to be working, but it probably needs more testing:

```python
def fixup_scripts(home_dir):
    if sys.platform == 'win32':
        bin_suffix = 'Scripts'
        # This is what we'll put:
        new_shebang = '#!%s /c python' % os.path.normcase(os.environ['COMSPEC'])
    else:
        bin_suffix = 'bin'
        # This is what we'll put:
        new_shebang = '#!/usr/bin/env python%s' % sys.version[:3]
    # This is what we expect at the top of scripts:
    shebang = '#!%s' % os.path.normcase(os.path.abspath(os.path.join(home_dir, bin_suffix, 'python')))

    activate = "import os; activate_this=os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__)), 'activate_this.py'); execfile(activate_this, dict(__file__=activate_this)); del os, activate_this"
    bin_dir = os.path.join(home_dir, bin_suffix)
    home_dir, lib_dir, inc_dir, bin_dir = path_locations(home_dir)
    for filename in os.listdir(bin_dir):
        filename = os.path.join(bin_dir, filename)
        if not os.path.isfile(filename):
            # ignore subdirs, e.g. .svn ones.
            continue
        f = open(filename, 'rb')
        try:
            try:
                lines = f.read().decode('utf-8').splitlines()
            except UnicodeDecodeError:
                # This is probably a binary program instead
                # of a script, so just ignore it.
                continue
        finally:
            f.close()
        if not lines:
            logger.warn('Script %s is an empty file' % filename)
            continue
        old_shebang = lines[0].strip()
        old_shebang = old_shebang[0:2] + os.path.normcase(old_shebang[2:])
        if not old_shebang.startswith(shebang):
            if os.path.basename(filename) in OK_ABS_SCRIPTS:
                logger.debug('Cannot make script %s relative' % filename)
            elif lines[0].strip() == new_shebang:
                logger.info('Script %s has already been made relative' % filename)
            else:
                logger.warn('Script %s cannot be made relative (it\'s not a normal script that starts with %s)'
                            % (filename, shebang))
            continue
        logger.notify('Making script %s relative' % filename)
        lines = [new_shebang+'\n', activate+'\n'] + lines[1:]
        f = open(filename, 'wb')
        f.write(os.linesep.join(lines).encode('utf-8'))
        f.close()
```
